title: The UK and International Institutions
overview: The United Kingdom works closely with other nations to promote peace, security, and prosperity. Through international organisations, Britain plays an active role in solving global problems, supporting development, and protecting human rights.

study_sections.heading: The United Nations (UN)
study_sections.content: The United Nations was founded in 1945 to maintain peace after WWII. The UK is a founding member and one of five permanent members of the UN Security Council. British peacekeepers and aid workers serve in UN programmes worldwide.

study_sections.heading: The Commonwealth
study_sections.content: The Commonwealth of Nations includes 56 independent countries. The UK monarch is the symbolic Head. Members work together to promote democracy, development, and human rights through cooperation and friendship.

study_sections.heading: NATO
study_sections.content: NATO was established in 1949 for collective defence—an attack on one is an attack on all. The UK is a founding member and contributes to peacekeeping and security missions.

study_sections.heading: World Trade Organization
study_sections.content: The WTO helps nations trade fairly and settle disputes. The UK works to ensure open and fair global trade.

study_sections.heading: The Council of Europe
study_sections.content: Created in 1949 to protect human rights in Europe. The European Convention on Human Rights inspired the UK Human Rights Act 1998.

study_sections.heading: UK Global Role
study_sections.content: Britain supports global stability through diplomacy, development aid, and peacekeeping via the Foreign, Commonwealth and Development Office (FCDO).

key_facts: The UK works through international organisations to promote peace, security, human rights, and cooperation.
The United Nations, founded in 1945, aims to maintain global peace and security.
The UK is a permanent member of the UN Security Council with power to veto resolutions.
British forces, police, and diplomats support UN peacekeeping, humanitarian, and development missions.
The Commonwealth links more than 50 independent countries, with the British monarch as Head of the Commonwealth.
Commonwealth cooperation supports democracy, development, legal exchange, and events such as the Commonwealth Games.
NATO, created in 1949, provides collective defence for members under Article 5 of the Washington Treaty.
The Council of Europe, founded in 1949, promotes human rights and democracy across Europe.
The European Convention on Human Rights is enforced by the European Court of Human Rights in Strasbourg.
The Human Rights Act 1998 makes Convention rights part of UK law.
The World Trade Organization sets rules for global trade, while the UK also plays roles in the G7 and G20.
The Foreign, Commonwealth & Development Office manages UK diplomacy, aid, and consular support overseas.
British cultural bodies such as the BBC World Service and British Council share language, education, and culture internationally.
memory_hook: Think of the UK’s global work as a web—UN, NATO, Commonwealth, and rights courts forming strands that hold international cooperation together.

questions.prompt: When was the United Nations founded?
questions.explanation: The UN was established in 1945 after WWII.

questions.prompt: How many permanent members does the UN Security Council have?
questions.explanation: The UK is one of five permanent members.

questions.prompt: How many countries are Commonwealth members?
Who is Head of the Commonwealth?
questions.explanation: The UK Monarch is symbolic Head.

options: Mutual defence—attack on one is attack on all

flashcards: When was the United Nations founded?
How many permanent UN Security Council members are there?
Five
Which countries are permanent Security Council members?
China, France, Russia, the UK, the USA
When was NATO founded?
What is NATO's collective defence principle?
Attack on one, attack on all
How many Commonwealth countries are there?
Fifty-six
Who is the Head of the Commonwealth?
The Monarch
When was the Commonwealth Charter signed?
What does WTO stand for?
World Trade Organization
What is the WTO's purpose?
To promote fair global trade
When was the Council of Europe created?
What is the Council of Europe's mission?
Protect human rights and democracy
Which treaty protects rights across Europe?
European Convention on Human Rights
Which UK law embeds Convention rights?
Human Rights Act 1998
What does FCDO stand for?
Foreign, Commonwealth and Development Office
What is the FCDO's main role?
Managing foreign policy and aid
What is the UK's international broadcaster?
BBC World Service
What organisation promotes UK culture overseas?
British Council
